q: The three critical research areas of biotechnology described in NCERT include providing the best catalyst, creating optimal conditions and:
opts: Downstream processing technologies to purify the product
exp: A complete biotechnology process also requires recovery and purification of the product.

q: A genetically modified organism is one whose:
opts: Phenotype changes only because of nutrition
Genetic material has been altered using genetic engineering
Chromosome number changes naturally in meiosis
Cells are grown only in tissue culture
exp: GM organisms contain deliberately altered genetic material.

q: Which is a stated advantage of genetically modified crops?
exp: NCERT lists reduced post-harvest losses among the benefits of GM crops.

q: GM crops can improve food quality by:
exp: Biofortification through genetic engineering can improve nutritional quality.

q: A major ecological objective of pest-resistant GM crops is to:
exp: In-built resistance can reduce pesticide application.

q: Tolerance to abiotic stresses in GM plants may include tolerance to:
exp: Abiotic stresses include drought, salinity, heat and cold.

q: The use of genetically modified plants to reduce post-harvest losses primarily improves:
exp: Reduced spoilage allows a larger fraction of production to reach consumers.

q: A crop engineered to use minerals more efficiently could help prevent:
exp: NCERT notes improved mineral-use efficiency as a possible GM benefit.

q: Which statement is most accurate regarding GM plants?
opts: Every GM crop has the same inserted gene
Different GM crops are designed for different useful traits
exp: The engineered trait depends on the gene and intended application.

q: The bacterium that provides many Bt toxin genes is:
exp: B. thuringiensis produces insecticidal crystal proteins.

q: Bt in Bt cotton stands for:
exp: The name refers to the source bacterium of cry genes.

q: The insecticidal protein of Bacillus thuringiensis is produced in the bacterium as:
exp: The crystal protein is synthesised as an inactive protoxin.

q: Bt protoxin becomes active in the insect gut because of:
exp: The alkaline gut environment solubilises and activates the protoxin.

q: After activation, Bt toxin primarily acts by:
opts: Blocking DNA replication in plant cells
Binding to midgut epithelial cells and forming pores
exp: Pore formation causes cell swelling, lysis and insect death.

q: Bt toxin kills susceptible insects mainly due to damage to the:
exp: Activated toxin damages cells lining the insect midgut.

q: The cry genes encode:
exp: Cry genes code for Bt crystal proteins.

q: Different cry genes are useful because they:
opts: All kill exactly the same insect
May be toxic to different groups of insects
exp: Different Cry proteins have different insect specificities.

q: The genes cryIAc and cryIIAb have been used to control:
exp: NCERT links these cry genes with cotton bollworm control.

q: The gene cryIAb is commonly associated with resistance to:
exp: CryIAb is effective against corn borer.

q: A Bt crop does not normally harm its own cells because the toxin is:
opts: Specific to susceptible insect gut receptors and conditions
Active only in acidic plant vacuoles
exp: Toxicity depends on activation and receptor binding in susceptible insects.

q: An insect feeding on Bt cotton dies after the toxin:
opts: Enters its alkaline gut and is activated
exp: Gut alkalinity activates the protoxin, which then damages epithelial cells.

q: Which sequence correctly represents Bt toxin action?
opts: Pore formation → ingestion → activation → receptor binding
Ingestion → activation → receptor binding → pore formation
Activation in plant → ingestion → DNA cleavage
Receptor binding → gene insertion → pore formation
exp: The insect ingests protoxin, which is activated, binds receptors and forms pores.

q: The Bt toxin crystals are harmless to Bacillus thuringiensis because they are:
exp: The bacterium stores the protein in an inactive crystalline form.

q: A crop carrying a cry gene is best described as:
opts: A transgenic crop with insect resistance
A hybrid produced only by crossing
A crop resistant to every pathogen
exp: Insertion and expression of a cry gene create transgenic insect resistance.

q: A major benefit of Bt cotton is reduction in the use of:
exp: Bt cotton reduces insecticide use for susceptible target pests.

q: Resistance management in Bt crops is important because:
opts: Target insects can evolve resistant alleles
Bt genes always disappear in one generation
exp: Strong selection can increase resistance alleles in pest populations.

q: Planting non-Bt refuge plants near Bt crops can help by:
opts: Maintaining susceptible insects that mate with resistant survivors
Stopping gene expression in Bt plants
exp: Refuges slow resistance evolution by preserving susceptible alleles.

q: RNA interference is a method of:
opts: Silencing a specific gene using complementary RNA
exp: RNAi reduces expression of a target gene through sequence-specific RNA.

q: RNA interference operates in all eukaryotic organisms primarily as a mechanism of:
exp: NCERT describes RNAi as a cellular defence mechanism.

q: RNAi can be triggered by:
exp: Double-stranded RNA initiates sequence-specific silencing.

q: In RNAi, the silencing RNA is complementary to:
exp: Complementary interaction prevents expression of the target mRNA.

q: The nematode controlled through RNAi in transgenic tobacco is:
exp: M. incognita infects tobacco roots and was targeted by RNAi.

q: Meloidogyne incognita causes damage primarily by infecting:
exp: It is a root-knot nematode.

q: The vector used to introduce nematode-specific genes into tobacco was based on:
exp: Agrobacterium vectors delivered sequences producing complementary RNAs.

q: The transgenic tobacco plant produced both sense and antisense RNA. These RNAs:
exp: Complementary sense and antisense transcripts hybridised to form dsRNA.

q: Double-stranded RNA in the transgenic plant caused:
opts: Silencing of a specific nematode mRNA
Conversion of RNA to protein without ribosomes
exp: The dsRNA triggered RNAi against the complementary nematode transcript.

q: The nematode could not survive in the transgenic host because:
opts: Its essential gene expression was silenced
exp: RNAi blocked an essential target transcript in the parasite.

q: Which pair is correctly matched?
opts: Golden rice - Bt toxin against bollworm
exp: The NCERT RNAi example uses transgenic tobacco against M. incognita.

q: RNAi is highly specific because it depends on:
exp: Base pairing allows selective recognition of target RNA.

q: A gene-silencing construct containing inverted complementary sequences is designed mainly to produce:
exp: Complementary transcripts can fold or hybridise into dsRNA.

q: Recombinant therapeutics are preferred over similar products isolated from non-human sources because they:
exp: Human-compatible recombinant proteins can be safer and more consistent.

q: The first genetically engineered human insulin was prepared commercially by:
exp: Eli Lilly produced recombinant human insulin in 1983.

q: Human insulin consists of:
opts: Two polypeptide chains, A and B
A DNA and an RNA chain
exp: Mature insulin contains A and B polypeptide chains linked by disulfide bonds.

q: The A and B chains of insulin are linked by:
exp: Disulfide bridges stabilise mature insulin.

q: In mammals, insulin is initially synthesised as:
opts: Proinsulin containing an additional C peptide
Two separate mature chains in different cells
exp: Proinsulin includes the connecting C peptide.

q: The C peptide is absent from:
exp: C peptide is removed during maturation of proinsulin.

q: The main challenge in producing human insulin in bacteria was that bacteria:
opts: Do not naturally process proinsulin exactly as human cells do
exp: The bacterial system does not perform normal human proinsulin processing.

q: Eli Lilly produced human insulin by synthesising DNA sequences for:
exp: A- and B-chain coding sequences were expressed separately in E. coli.

q: After separate production in bacteria, insulin A and B chains were:
exp: The purified chains were combined to form functional insulin.

q: Recombinant human insulin is also called:
exp: Humulin is recombinant human insulin.

q: Earlier insulin preparations obtained from cattle and pigs sometimes caused:
exp: Differences in animal insulin could trigger immune responses.

q: Which component is removed while converting proinsulin to mature insulin?
exp: The connecting peptide is cleaved during maturation.

q: If a recombinant bacterial product contains insulin A, B and C regions in one chain, the immediate product most closely resembles:
exp: Presence of C peptide indicates proinsulin-like structure.

q: A person lacking functional insulin can be treated by recombinant DNA technology through production of:
exp: Recombinant insulin is a therapeutic peptide hormone.

q: Gene therapy aims to:
opts: Replace every cell of the body
Correct or compensate for a defective gene
exp: A normal functional gene is introduced to restore missing activity.

q: The first clinical gene therapy mentioned in NCERT was given in 1990 to a child with:
exp: The first gene therapy case involved adenosine deaminase deficiency.

q: ADA deficiency affects the:
exp: ADA deficiency causes severe immune dysfunction.

q: ADA deficiency may be treated by:
opts: Bone marrow transplantation, enzyme replacement or gene therapy
exp: NCERT describes all three approaches.

q: In ex vivo ADA gene therapy, lymphocytes are:
opts: Removed, genetically corrected and returned to the patient
exp: Patient lymphocytes are cultured, modified and reinfused.

q: A functional ADA cDNA is introduced into lymphocytes using:
exp: Retroviral vectors can deliver the functional gene to cultured cells.

q: Gene therapy using genetically modified lymphocytes may need repeated treatment because:
opts: All vectors are destroyed before entry
exp: Corrected lymphocytes have a limited lifespan.

q: A potentially permanent cure for ADA deficiency would require introduction of the functional gene into:
opts: Cells at an early embryonic stage or suitable stem cells
exp: Long-lived progenitor cells can pass the correction to descendant cells.

q: Enzyme replacement therapy for ADA deficiency involves giving the patient:
exp: The missing enzyme can be supplied, but repeated administration is needed.

q: Which treatment modifies a patient's own cells outside the body?
exp: Cells are removed, engineered and returned.

q: A normal gene introduced into a patient is intended primarily to:
exp: The therapeutic gene restores a missing or defective function.

q: Molecular diagnosis is especially useful because it can detect disease:
opts: At an early stage, sometimes before symptoms
exp: PCR and related methods detect low amounts of pathogen or abnormal genes.

q: Conventional diagnostic methods may fail early in infection because:
opts: Pathogen concentration may be too low
exp: Molecular amplification can reveal very small amounts of nucleic acid.

q: PCR assists diagnosis by:
opts: Amplifying a specific nucleic acid sequence
Counting chromosomes under a microscope only
exp: PCR makes many copies of a target DNA sequence.

q: PCR can help detect HIV by amplifying:
exp: Specific viral sequences can be detected even at low abundance.

q: PCR is useful in cancer diagnosis because it can detect:
exp: Specific genetic alterations can be amplified and identified.

q: A radioactive single-stranded DNA or RNA molecule used to identify a complementary sequence is called a:
exp: A labelled probe hybridises with its complementary target.

q: A probe detects a target sequence through:
exp: Hybridisation depends on sequence complementarity.

q: In autoradiography, a clone carrying a mutated gene may fail to appear if:
opts: The labelled probe does not hybridise to the altered sequence
exp: A sequence change can prevent complementary probe binding.

q: ELISA is based on the principle of:
exp: ELISA detects antigens or antibodies using specific immune binding.

q: ELISA may detect infection by identifying:
exp: Both antigen detection and antibody detection formats are possible.

q: Which method is most directly suited for amplifying a minute amount of pathogen DNA?
exp: PCR exponentially amplifies target DNA.

q: Which method directly depends on antigen-antibody specificity?
exp: ELISA is an immunological assay.

q: A patient has too little viral DNA for direct visual detection. The best first step is:
exp: PCR raises the target DNA concentration to detectable levels.

q: A labelled probe will bind most strongly to:
opts: Any protein with the same mass
exp: Stable hybridisation requires complementary bases.

q: Molecular diagnosis can identify a genetic disorder by detecting:
exp: Sequence-specific methods can reveal disease-causing mutations.

q: Animals whose genome has been deliberately modified to carry a foreign gene are called:
exp: A transgene is introduced and maintained in the animal genome.

q: The majority of existing transgenic animals are:
exp: NCERT states that over 95% of transgenic animals are mice.

q: Transgenic animals are used to study:
exp: Gene regulation and developmental roles can be analysed in whole animals.

q: A transgenic animal carrying an altered gene can help determine:
opts: How that gene contributes to normal biological function
exp: Changing gene activity reveals its physiological role.

q: Transgenic disease models are created mainly to:
opts: Understand disease mechanisms and test treatments
exp: Engineered animals can mimic human diseases.

q: Examples of diseases studied using transgenic animal models include:
opts: Cancer, cystic fibrosis and rheumatoid arthritis
exp: NCERT lists several human disorders modelled in animals.

q: A transgenic cow named Rosie produced milk enriched with:
exp: Rosie's milk contained human alpha-lactalbumin.

q: Rosie produced human protein-enriched milk in:
exp: NCERT records Rosie as the first transgenic cow producing such milk in 1997.

q: The milk produced by Rosie was nutritionally more balanced for human babies because it contained:
exp: The introduced human milk protein improved nutritional suitability.

q: Transgenic animals can produce biological products by:
opts: Expressing useful human proteins in milk or other tissues
exp: Animals can function as bioreactors for therapeutic proteins.

q: Before a vaccine is used in humans, its safety may be tested in:
exp: Transgenic models can support vaccine safety assessment.

q: Transgenic mice have been used to test the safety of:
exp: NCERT cites polio vaccine safety testing in transgenic mice.

q: Chemical safety testing on transgenic animals is also called:
exp: Animals can be engineered to respond sensitively to toxic substances.

q: Transgenic animals made more sensitive to toxic substances allow researchers to:
opts: Obtain toxicity results in less time
exp: Enhanced sensitivity can reveal toxic effects rapidly.

q: Which is not a major use of transgenic animals listed in NCERT?
exp: Chemical fertiliser manufacture is not one of the listed uses.

q: A transgenic model of a human disease is valuable when it:
opts: Reproduces relevant features of the disease
exp: Similarity to human pathology enables mechanistic and therapeutic research.

q: The term 'biological products' in transgenic-animal applications refers mainly to:
opts: Useful proteins or therapeutics produced by living systems
exp: Engineered animals can synthesise valuable biomolecules.

q: A scientist introduces a human protein gene into a goat so that the protein is secreted in milk. This is an example of:
exp: The animal is used as a living production system.

q: An animal engineered to carry an oncogene is most directly useful as a:
exp: Such animals can model tumour development.

q: Which comparison is correct?
opts: PCR amplifies DNA; transgenic animals model gene function in whole organisms
ELISA amplifies DNA; PCR produces milk proteins
RNAi creates antibodies; probes produce insulin
exp: The paired functions are accurate.

q: The organisation mentioned in NCERT that evaluates the validity of GM research and safety of introducing GM organisms for public use is:
exp: GEAC evaluates biosafety and proposed release of GM organisms.

q: GEAC is concerned particularly with:
exp: Its role includes evaluation of GM research and environmental/public safety.

q: Ethical standards are required in biotechnology because genetic modification may affect:
opts: Humans, other organisms and the environment
exp: Biotechnology can have broad biological and social consequences.

q: A patent grants the inventor:
opts: Legal rights over an invention for a limited period
Permanent ownership of all natural organisms
exp: Patents provide time-limited legal protection for qualifying inventions.

q: A biopatent is a patent related to:
exp: Biopatents concern biological materials and biotechnology inventions.

q: Biopiracy refers to:
opts: Unauthorised use of bioresources and traditional knowledge without fair compensation
exp: It involves exploitation without proper permission or benefit sharing.

q: Developing nations are often rich in:
exp: Their biological resources and indigenous knowledge may attract commercial interest.

q: A foreign company patents a product developed from an indigenous medicinal practice without acknowledging or compensating the community. This is:
exp: Traditional knowledge has been commercially appropriated without fair benefit sharing.

q: The turmeric patent controversy is associated with claims involving:
exp: Traditional Indian knowledge of turmeric's wound-healing use was central to the dispute.

q: The basmati controversy involved intellectual property claims related to:
exp: Basmati germplasm and characteristic traits raised biopiracy concerns.

q: The neem controversy is commonly linked with patents concerning:
opts: Pesticidal or antifungal uses derived from neem
exp: Traditional uses of neem prompted challenges to certain foreign patent claims.

q: Fair benefit sharing means that:
opts: Communities providing resources or knowledge receive appropriate recognition and returns
exp: Equitable sharing addresses ethical use of biological resources.

q: Approval of a GM crop should consider:
opts: Food safety, environmental effects and gene flow
exp: Risk assessment must consider health and ecological consequences.

q: Uncontrolled transfer of a transgene to wild relatives is a concern because it may:
exp: Gene flow can affect wild populations and ecosystems.

q: The need for regulation of biotechnology arises because:
opts: Powerful applications may carry health, environmental and ethical risks
exp: Regulation balances benefits with responsible risk management.

q: Consider the following statements about Bt toxin: 1. It is produced as an inactive protoxin. 2. It is activated in the alkaline insect gut. 3. It forms pores in midgut epithelial cells. Which are correct?
exp: All three describe the NCERT mechanism of Bt toxin.

q: Consider the following statements about cry genes: 1. All cry genes target exactly the same insect. 2. cryIAc and cryIIAb are used against cotton bollworms. 3. cryIAb is useful against corn borer. Which are correct?
exp: Cry proteins differ in specificity; statements 2 and 3 are correct.

q: Consider the following statements about RNAi: 1. It is sequence-specific. 2. It is triggered by double-stranded RNA. 3. It enhances translation of the target mRNA. Which are correct?
exp: RNAi silences rather than enhances target expression.

q: Consider the following statements about recombinant insulin: 1. Mature insulin contains A and B chains. 2. Proinsulin contains C peptide. 3. C peptide remains in mature insulin. Which are correct?
exp: C peptide is removed during maturation.

q: Consider the following statements about ADA gene therapy: 1. Patient lymphocytes may be modified ex vivo. 2. Corrected lymphocytes may require periodic reinfusion. 3. Mature erythrocytes are the ideal permanent target. Which are correct?
exp: Lymphocytes have limited lifespan; mature red cells are unsuitable permanent targets.

q: Consider the following statements: 1. PCR amplifies target DNA. 2. ELISA relies on antigen-antibody interaction. 3. A nucleic acid probe binds through complementary base pairing. Which are correct?
exp: All statements accurately describe molecular diagnostic tools.

q: Consider the following statements about transgenic animals: 1. They can model human diseases. 2. They may produce useful biological products. 3. They are never used in safety testing. Which are correct?
exp: Transgenic animals are also used in vaccine and chemical safety testing.

q: Consider the following statements about Rosie: 1. She was a transgenic cow. 2. Her milk contained human alpha-lactalbumin. 3. She produced Bt toxin in milk. Which are correct?
exp: The Bt-toxin statement is incorrect.

q: Consider the following statements about biopiracy: 1. It may involve traditional knowledge. 2. It may occur without fair compensation. 3. It is identical to approved gene therapy. Which are correct?
exp: Biopiracy concerns unfair appropriation, not medical gene therapy.

q: Consider the following statements about GM crops: 1. They may reduce post-harvest losses. 2. They may improve mineral-use efficiency. 3. Every GM crop produces Bt toxin. Which are correct?
exp: GM traits vary; not all GM crops are Bt crops.

q: Statement I: Bt protoxin is activated in the insect gut. Statement II: The insect gut provides an alkaline environment that solubilises the crystal protein.
opts: Both statements are true, and II explains I
Both are true, but II does not explain I
I is true, II is false
I is false, II is true
exp: Alkaline pH is central to activation.

q: Statement I: Mature insulin contains C peptide. Statement II: C peptide is removed during proinsulin processing.
opts: I is true, II is false
I is false, II is true
exp: C peptide is present in proinsulin but absent from mature insulin.

q: Statement I: ELISA can be used to detect an infection. Statement II: ELISA may identify either an antigen or antibodies produced against it.
opts: Both statements are true, and II explains I
Both are true, but II does not explain I
I is true, II is false
I is false, II is true
exp: Antigen-antibody detection forms the diagnostic basis.

q: Statement I: Transgenic mice may be used in vaccine safety testing. Statement II: All vaccine testing can therefore be done without any further evaluation.
opts: I is true, II is false
I is false, II is true
exp: Animal testing is one stage, not the entire evaluation process.

q: Statement I: Patents and biopiracy are synonymous. Statement II: A valid patent is a legal right, whereas biopiracy involves unfair or unauthorised appropriation.
opts: I is true, II is false
I is false, II is true
exp: The concepts are distinct.

q: Assertion: Bt toxin is not active inside Bacillus thuringiensis. Reason: It is synthesised as an inactive protoxin.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The protoxin form protects the bacterium from its own insecticidal protein.

q: Assertion: Different cry genes may be used against different insect pests. Reason: Cry proteins show target specificity.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Specificity explains why different genes are chosen for different pests.

q: Assertion: RNA interference can protect a transgenic plant against a parasite. Reason: Complementary double-stranded RNA can silence an essential parasite gene.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Sequence-specific silencing prevents parasite survival.

q: Assertion: Animal insulin could sometimes provoke immune reactions in humans. Reason: Animal insulin may differ slightly in amino acid sequence from human insulin.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Sequence differences can be immunogenic.

q: Assertion: Corrected lymphocyte therapy for ADA deficiency may not be permanent. Reason: Lymphocytes have a finite lifespan.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Loss of corrected cells can necessitate repeated infusions.

q: Assertion: PCR is useful for early diagnosis. Reason: It can amplify very small quantities of target DNA.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Amplification enables detection before pathogen load becomes high.

q: Assertion: ELISA directly amplifies pathogen DNA. Reason: ELISA is based on antigen-antibody interaction.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The assertion is false; the reason is true.

q: Assertion: Transgenic animals can be used to study normal gene function. Reason: Altering expression of a gene can reveal its physiological role.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Phenotypic consequences help infer function.

q: Assertion: Biopiracy raises ethical concerns. Reason: Biological resources or traditional knowledge may be commercially used without fair benefit sharing.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The reason states the ethical problem.

q: Assertion: Every genetically modified organism is automatically safe for unrestricted release. Reason: GM organisms require biosafety evaluation before public use.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The assertion is false and the reason is true.

q: Match the gene/product with target or role: I. cryIAc; II. cryIAb; III. dsRNA; IV. ADA cDNA. a. Corn borer; b. Cotton bollworm; c. Gene silencing; d. Correction of ADA deficiency
exp: The four pairs follow NCERT examples.

q: Match the biotechnology product with its source/process: I. Humulin; II. Bt toxin; III. Human alpha-lactalbumin in milk; IV. RNAi-resistant tobacco. a. Transgenic cow Rosie; b. Recombinant E. coli insulin chains; c. cry gene expression; d. Sense and antisense RNA
exp: Each product or trait is matched with its production strategy.

q: Match the diagnostic method with principle: I. PCR; II. ELISA; III. Nucleic acid probe; IV. Autoradiography. a. Antigen-antibody binding; b. Amplification; c. Complementary hybridisation; d. Detection of radioactive signal
exp: These are the defining principles of the methods.

q: Match the transgenic-animal use with example: I. Disease model; II. Biological product; III. Vaccine safety; IV. Chemical safety. a. Rosie; b. Polio vaccine testing in mice; c. Cancer model; d. Enhanced sensitivity to toxic substances
exp: The examples correspond to the four NCERT uses.

q: Match the term with description: I. Biopatent; II. Biopiracy; III. GEAC; IV. Benefit sharing. a. Regulatory biosafety evaluation; b. Legal protection of biological invention; c. Equitable returns to knowledge/resource providers; d. Unfair appropriation
exp: Each term is paired with its correct meaning.

q: Match the insulin-related term with description: I. Proinsulin; II. Mature insulin; III. C peptide; IV. Humulin. a. Removed during processing; b. Recombinant human insulin; c. A and B chains plus connecting segment; d. A and B chains linked by disulfide bonds
exp: The structural and therapeutic terms are correctly paired.

q: Match the application with organism: I. Bt cotton; II. RNAi tobacco; III. Recombinant insulin production; IV. Rosie. a. E. coli; b. Transgenic cow; c. Bacillus-derived cry gene; d. Meloidogyne resistance
exp: Each application is linked to its key organism or biological source.

q: Match the example with issue/application: I. Turmeric; II. Basmati; III. Neem; IV. ADA deficiency. a. Gene therapy; b. Wound-healing patent dispute; c. Rice intellectual-property controversy; d. Pesticidal-use patent controversy
exp: The first three are biopiracy/patent examples; ADA is a gene-therapy example.

q: Match the step with outcome in Bt action: I. Ingestion; II. Alkaline solubilisation; III. Receptor binding; IV. Pore formation. a. Entry into insect gut; b. Protoxin activation; c. Attachment to midgut epithelium; d. Cell swelling and lysis
exp: This is the correct mechanistic sequence.

q: Match the therapy/tool with limitation or strength: I. Enzyme replacement for ADA; II. Lymphocyte gene therapy; III. PCR; IV. ELISA. a. May require repeated enzyme doses; b. May require repeated cell infusions; c. Detects minute DNA amounts; d. Detects antigen-antibody interaction
exp: The pairs summarise key advantages or limitations.
